item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of my mild frustrations is that a work like this, when received by an internet audience of primarily working young people mostly steeped in genre fiction, is seen as extremely avant-garde when in fact it's a pale imitation of a now elderly poetic form first invented in the early 80s. What we see as the avant-garde is now hopelessly out of date. Bernstein is 70. David Antin is dead. Kenneth Goldsmith's post-digital internet utopianism became obsolete in 2015 when he was cancelled for turning Michael Brown's autopsy report into a poem. The days of looking to Twitter for art are long rotted six years on since. And it's very hard to try and find new ways of looking at the world in a post-post-digital humanistic landscape whose intelligentsia are more likely to have Invader Zim as a reference point to glom onto than any sort of brand new literary advancements, mostly as a result of childhood nostalgia steeped in the trauma of living in this late-capitalist hellscape.

This is not a criticism strictly speaking of the wiki's userbase, who are intelligent writers and readers, it's more an indictment of myself. Regardless of this poem's actual artistic worth or value as an SCP object/story, it was very stupid of me to parade it out and expect a ticker tape parade for my genius, if only because it's not very good and no one, me included, would be anywhere near the intellectual circles required to point that out with any serious authority. I just have a chronic illness where combining the SCP format, normally the denizen of funnee peanut memes and clickbait Youtube videos for children when it isn't left-wing internet urban fantasy/horror stories, with avant garde writings is endlessly fascinating. The sooner I grow out of it the smarter I will be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
William S. Burroughs stared in both Drugstore Cowboy and a Nike ad.

I assume you meant "starred"?

Overall, even if I had to reread a few times, I like it a lot. The format definitely works here. Even if it's a bit hard to parse, it's very intriguing. +1
Still trying to wrap my head around it, but maybe that's what you meant when you said poetry is hard to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: on second read, the arc is actually pretty much there. easy to miss the forest for the trees. still keeping the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm very happy to see some more poetry.

As you stated: poetry is difficult to contain
Yes, it is. It is also hard to execute. Good job. I also liked the formatting you used.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, these are great!

EDIT: except for esoteric-class, this thing is Keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's bits and pieces here that remind of Beckett, so of course, I upvoted. In all serious, I thought this was delightful and well deserving of its place onsite.

Sidenote, I'm trying my hand at tagging, would 'cognitohazard concept esoteric-class language meta poetry' be appropriate, in your opinion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1.

  • because it's poetry and I have a soft spot for poetry
  • because Egoism is cool even if it's not really sustainable
  • because you're saying Fuck You to post-modern art using post-post-modern art

• because i like it and that's what poetry is all about.

Also I don't know why but various parts of this gave me big Persona 3 and Fluxus vibes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's actually a huge honor to hear you say this fulfills the goal of being a language poem

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's not as deep as it pretends to be (or so i am pretending) but i enjoyed some parts of it

i think it is difficult to offer criticism of this on a meaningful level precisely because it wants to reject the poem as turns of meaning. instead, by playing with the poem as turns of text/language (which some call truth), it kind of absolves itself of the need to be read in that way?

i would even fault this for the lack of an arc but i do not think the l=a=n=g=u=a=g=e poets would say it in that way

there is no way that this piece is doing a bad job because it is doing precisely its job. this piece is not doing a good job either because its definition of job prevents my measuring as such. the truer thing to judge this on is whether I like it, and I do, and that's truer than text i guess

+1

item.title: NOW THAT'S WHAT I CALL A CRY PILLOW Vol. 45
item.content:encoded:
Hi, this is a poem about not understanding, and as thus, I suspect the audience might not understand it. As much as it is an homage to the obtuseness of Mid-century American poets like Bernstein, Antin, Olson, and Ginsberg, it is still mostly meant to be understandable as a mainlist article.

This was also election week therapy.

TL;DR: It's a bog-standard Series I item (with a touch of Series III terminology) filtered through seventy years of American poetry.
